content.text: Who Does What in the Pharmacy?

content.body: A well-functioning pharmacy depends on a team of professionals, each with clearly defined roles and responsibilities. Understanding these roles — and crucially, the legal boundaries between them — is fundamental to safe practice. In the Caribbean, these boundaries vary by jurisdiction, making it essential for technicians to know exactly what they can and cannot do in their specific island context.

rows: Bachelor's or Doctor of Pharmacy (PharmD), registered with national pharmacy board
Final verification of prescriptions, clinical consultations, drug therapy management, supervision of staff
Yes — final checks and patient counselling
Official training programme, Certification where required
Prescription data entry, medication preparation, inventory management, assisting with picking/packaging, and dispensing under supervision
Preparation yes; final check requires pharmacist oversight in most Caribbean jurisdictions
On-the-job training, no formal certification required in most jurisdictions
Stocking shelves, cashier duties, answering phones, basic customer service

content.body: Never exceed the scope of practice defined by your island's pharmacy legislation. Performing acts reserved for pharmacists — such as independently verifying prescriptions or providing clinical advice — can result in legal prosecution, even if done with good intentions. When in doubt, always defer to the supervising pharmacist.
content.title: Critical Legal Point

content.text: The Pharmacy Technician as a Professional

content.body: Being a pharmacy technician is not merely a job — it is a profession with ethical obligations, continuing education requirements, and a duty of care to every patient. As a technician, you are often the first person a patient interacts with. Your accuracy in preparing medications, your attention to detail in managing inventory, and your professionalism in every interaction directly impact patient health outcomes.

items: Accuracy: A single dispensing error can cause a patient serious harm or even death
Confidentiality: Patient information must be protected at all times
Professionalism: Your conduct reflects on the entire pharmacy profession
Continuous Learning: Drug information changes constantly — staying current is a duty, not optional
Teamwork: Effective communication with pharmacists and other healthcare providers is essential

content.body: The pharmacy team consists of specialized professionals who collaborate to ensure safe and effective medication distribution and patient care. Key roles include the Pharmacist, who oversees clinical safety, and the Pharmacy Technicians and Assistants, who handle dispensing, inventory, and front-line customer service.
The lead of the pharmacy, responsible for verifying prescriptions, monitoring for drug interactions, and providing patient consultations.

items: Medication Review: Assesses the appropriateness of a drug regimen, checking dosages and ensuring safety against allergies or conflicting medications.
Patient Education: Instructs patients on proper medication administration (e.g., how to use an inhaler) and potential side effects.
Clinical Services: Many pharmacists can now adapt prescriptions, manage chronic conditions, and administer point-of-care tests or immunizations like the flu shot.

content.body: Highly trained professionals who manage the operational and technical aspects of dispensing medicines.

items: Dispensing & Assembly: Prepares, measures, and accurately labels prescriptions under the supervision of a pharmacist.
Safety & Checking: Performs final checks on dispensed items and takes accountability for their own accurate practice.
Patient Counseling: May be qualified to conduct initial consultations or advise on over-the-counter (OTC) products.

content.body: Support staff who ensure the pharmacy runs smoothly behind the counter.

items: Inventory Management: Orders, receives, and stores pharmaceutical stock, and removes expired products.
Customer Support: Handles prescription intake and collection, cash transactions, and assists with basic over-the-counter inquiries.
